FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data (Years 1-3, 5.0 release), N=7514 adolescents ages 9-10 at baseline 2016-2018

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures social media time (hours per day) and problematic social media use (SMAQ). SMAQ is a qualifying problematic/addictive scale.

FT_IC3.evidence: Modified Poisson regression with social media time (IV) predicting alcohol sipping (DV) at Year 3; Risk Ratio 1.31 (95% CI 1.20-1.43), p<0.001

FT_IC4.evidence: Risk Ratio 1.31 (95% CI 1.20-1.43), p<0.001 reported in Table 2 Model 1; also mediation analysis with 25% indirect effect (p=0.001) in Table 3

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis; prospective mediation analysis with cross-sectional, longitudinal, and mediation modeling (GSEM)

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article (Substance Use & Misuse, 2024, 59:6, 971-976). No evidence of duplication.

FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes social media→alcohol (non-SM outcome) analysis, plus mediation analysis involving social media. Smartphone/SM is not solely a DV with demographic predictors. Does not apply.
notes: Clear INCLUDE. Prospective design with multiple inferential tests; SM and problematic SM are separate exposures; outcomes are non-SM (alcohol sipping).

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data (Years 2-3, 4.0 release), N=4,718 U.S. adolescents ages 10-15 years between 2018-2021

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures 'recreational screen time' (TV, movies, videos, video chat, video games, social media, texting). This is generic 'total screen time' composite reported with no separately extractable smartphone/SM coefficient. Does not meet QUALIFYING criteria for FT-IC2.

FT_IC3.evidence: Inferential tests exist (linear/logistic regression) but cannot evaluate without FT-IC2 MET

FT_IC4.evidence: Numerics reported (B, CI, p) but contingent on FT-IC2 failure

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD cross-sectional and categorical analyses of physical activity and screen time associations with CVD risk factors

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article (BMC Public Health, 2024, 24:1346). No evidence of duplication.

FT_EC7_reason: Not applicable; FT-IC2 NOT_MET; paper fails at exposure criterion (generic screen time composite, not phone/SM-specific)
notes: EXCLUDE via FT-EC2 (generic screen time composite per E2). Screen time is aggregate across TV, video games, social media, video chat, texting with no separate SM/phone extraction. Does not meet FT-IC2 QUALIFYING criteria.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data (baseline 2016-2018, two-year follow-up to Year 2), N=9,538 adolescents ages 9-10 at baseline

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures total screen time with specific breakdown of screen type components. Text explicitly reports associations for video chat, texting, videos, and video games separately; these are smartphone/social media qualifying exposures.

FT_IC3.evidence: Mixed-effects models with screen time predicting CBCL mental health symptom t-scores (depressive, conduct, somatic, ADHD, ODD). Example: depressive symptoms B=0.10, 95% CI 0.06-0.13, p<0.001

FT_IC4.evidence: Regression coefficients (B), 95% CIs, and p-values reported in Table 2 for screen time associations with CBCL DSM scales

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD prospective/longitudinal analysis of screen time at baseline predicting mental health at Year 2 follow-up

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article (BMC Public Health, 2024, 24:2686). No evidence of duplication.

FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes screen time→mental health (non-SM outcome) analyses. Screen time is IV; mental health symptoms are DVs. Not a case of demographic-only predictors of screen time. Does not apply.
notes: INCLUDE. Prospective design; screen time (including video chat, texting) measured at baseline predicts non-SM outcomes (mental health symptoms) at Year 2. Specific screen type breakdowns provided including phone/SM-relevant exposures.
